---
title: "Surfaces as discrete manifolds: the Laplace–Beltrami operator"
---

The Laplace--Beltrami operator $\Delta = M^{-1}L$ is the central object of the
surface half of this unit. It is the curved-surface analogue of the flat
Laplacian $\nabla^2$: at each point it reports how much a field there departs
from the average of the field in an infinitesimal neighbourhood, but with
"neighbourhood" and "average" measured *along a curved surface* rather than
across flat space. On a triangle mesh it factors cleanly into two pieces, and the
factorisation is the reason one operator serves so many tasks:

- a **cotangent stiffness** $L$, a sparse symmetric positive-semidefinite matrix
  whose weights $w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})$ encode the
  *shape* of the triangles - the local metric; and
- a **lumped mass** $M=\operatorname{diag}(A_i)$, the per-vertex area that
  converts the area-weighted stiffness into a true differential operator.

This single operator drives geodesic smoothing (this chapter), curvature
([next chapter](curvature.qmd#sec-mean)), and the surface spectrum
([the chapter after](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen)). In `nitrix` the stiffness and the
mass are deliberately *two symbols*, `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` and
`mesh_mass_matrix` / `vertex_areas`, that algorithms combine at the call site
($M^{-1}L$ for curvature, $M+tL$ for smoothing, the pencil $(L,M)$ for the
spectrum) rather than a single operator with a normalisation flag. We will see
why that separation is the right design.

The chapter follows [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-discretization) and
[the sparse operator substrate](operators.qmd#sec-ell). It assumes linear algebra
- sparse matrices, symmetry, positive-(semi)definiteness, solving $Ax=b$ - but no
prior differential geometry; everything is built from the flat Laplacian outward.

## A motivating problem {#sec-motivation}

Consider a reconstructed cortical surface, a triangle mesh of roughly $150{,}000$
vertices wrapping one hemisphere, carrying a noisy per-vertex scalar: a `sulc`
map, a myelin estimate, a cortical-thickness measurement, or the input to a
surface GLM. We wish to smooth it. The smoothing must run *along the surface*,
not through the surrounding three-dimensional space. Diffusion through space would
blur signal across the bank of a sulcus into the gyrus on the opposite bank -
points millimetres apart in $\mathbb{R}^3$ but centimetres apart as a geodesic
walker measures distance along the cortex. We want two points to share signal only
when they are close *along the sheet*.

This is intrinsically a geometric operation, and it exposes the operator we need.
To smooth along a surface one needs a notion of the local average of a field on a
curved sheet, and the discrepancy between a value and its local average is exactly
what a Laplacian reports. The question "how do I smooth on a surface?" is therefore
the question "what is the Laplacian of a triangle mesh?" Answering it yields, at no
extra cost, the operator behind curvature and the surface spectrum. We return to
the smoothing problem in @sec-smoothing once the operator is in hand.

## From the flat Laplacian to a curved surface {#sec-from-flat}

Begin with the familiar case. In flat space the Laplacian of a scalar field $f$ is
$\nabla^2 f = \partial^2_x f + \partial^2_y f + \cdots$. The value it returns at a
point has a meaning that survives every generalisation: $\nabla^2 f$ measures how
much $f$ at the point exceeds the average of $f$ over an infinitesimal
neighbourhood. At a local peak the neighbours are lower, the average sits below
$f$, and $\nabla^2 f < 0$; in a valley $\nabla^2 f > 0$; on a flat ramp the
surpluses and deficits cancel and $\nabla^2 f = 0$.

On a regular two-dimensional grid this "value minus neighbour average" is the
five-point stencil: a vertex, its four axis neighbours, weights $(-4,1,1,1,1)$.
The centre weight is minus the sum of the neighbour weights, so a constant field
returns exactly zero - the stencil sees only differences, never the absolute
level. This is the discrete echo of $\nabla^2\,\text{const}=0$, and we will require
it of every Laplacian we construct.

Moving onto a triangle mesh changes two things, and only two.

1. **The neighbourhood is irregular.** A vertex $i$ no longer has four tidy axis
   neighbours; it has its 1-ring $\mathcal{N}(i)$, the vertices sharing an edge
   with it - typically five or six on a cortical mesh, sometimes three, sometimes
   nine. The Laplacian must be a per-vertex sum over $\mathcal{N}(i)$, not a fixed
   stencil.
2. **The weights must encode shape.** On the grid every neighbour was the same
   distance away and unit weights were correct. On a mesh the triangles are
   stretched, sheared, and bent, and a neighbour across a long thin sliver should
   not count the same as a neighbour across a fat, well-shaped triangle. The
   weights must carry the *metric* - the local notion of length and angle that
   tells the discrete operator what the smooth surface underneath actually looks
   like.

The minimal construction ignores the second point: keep uniform weights and sum
over whatever neighbours exist,

$$
(L_{\text{unif}} f)[i] \;=\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} \bigl(f[i]-f[j]\bigr).
$$ {#eq-uniform}

This is the uniform (combinatorial, or graph) Laplacian. It is genuinely useful:
it is the operator behind `mesh_laplacian_smooth`
([operators](operators.qmd#sec-adjacency), revisited under
[graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-graph-laplacian)), which smooths *vertex
positions* by repeatedly nudging each vertex toward its neighbour mean. It depends
only on connectivity, never on coordinates, so it is cheap and robust.

It is also the first place the language of symmetry pays off. Both Laplacians we
will consider are **permutation equivariant**: relabel the vertices by a
permutation $P$ and the operator transforms as $L\mapsto PLP^\top$, so the
computed field is simply relabelled (the symmetry view of
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-transformations-bias)). That is the right
behaviour, and it holds for the uniform Laplacian too. What the uniform Laplacian
*lacks* is the second invariance we actually need. It depends on the mesh only
through its connectivity, so two different triangulations of the *same* smooth
surface produce *different* operators, and refining the mesh does not make
$L_{\text{unif}}$ approach the smooth $\nabla^2$ unless every triangle is
equilateral. It has confused the sampling pattern with the surface. The fix is to
make the weights depend on the metric, so that the operator becomes invariant to
the choice of triangulation and to rigid motion of the surface in space, changing
only when the *shape* changes. That operator is the cotangent Laplacian.

## The cotangent Laplacian {#sec-cotangent}

### The cotangent edge weight

The weight must answer one question: across the edge $(i,j)$, how strongly should
$f[i]$ and $f[j]$ be coupled? The coupling should be large when the two vertices
are well connected by the triangles between them - a fat, near-equilateral
triangle conducts a field smoothly from one vertex to the other - and small, even
zero, when they are joined only across a long, skinny sliver.

The quantity that captures this is the cotangent of the angle opposite the edge.
Picture the edge $(i,j)$ as the base of a triangle and the third vertex as the
apex with interior angle $\alpha$ opposite the base. When the apex angle is wide (a
fat triangle, $\alpha$ near $90^\circ$ or more) $\cot\alpha$ is small or negative,
giving weak coupling; when the apex angle is narrow (a sliver, the apex far from
the base) $\cot\alpha$ diverges toward $+\infty$, giving strong coupling. An
interior edge belongs to *two* triangles, contributing two opposite angles
$\alpha_{ij}$ and $\beta_{ij}$, so the symmetric edge weight is their average,

$$
w_{ij} \;=\; \tfrac12\bigl(\cot\alpha_{ij} + \cot\beta_{ij}\bigr).
$$ {#eq-cotweight}

A boundary edge has only one incident triangle and therefore a single cotangent -
relevant for an open patch, never for a closed cortical sphere.
@fig-lbo-cotangent shows the geometry and the behaviour of the weight as a triangle
degenerates.

The operator is then the same "value minus neighbour average" as @eq-uniform, now
weighted:

$$
(L f)[i] \;=\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij}\,\bigl(f[i]-f[j]\bigr),
\qquad
L_{ii} \;=\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} w_{ij}.
$$ {#eq-cotlap}

The off-diagonal entry at $(i,j)$ is $-w_{ij}$ and the diagonal $L_{ii}$ is the sum
of the row's edge weights. Because the diagonal equals the sum of the
off-diagonals, every row sums to zero, so $L\mathbf{1}=0$: constants lie in the null
space, $\Delta\,\text{const}=0$, the surface analogue of the grid stencil's
centre-minus-neighbours bookkeeping and the same zero-row-sum property required in
@sec-from-flat.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lbo-cotangent
#| fig-cap: "Left: an interior edge $(i,j)$ and the two angles $\\alpha,\\beta$ *opposite* it in the two incident triangles; the edge weight is $w_{ij}=\\tfrac12(\\cot\\alpha+\\cot\\beta)$. Right: the cotangent as a function of the opposite angle — it diverges to $+\\infty$ for a sliver (angle $\\to 0$) and goes negative past $90^\\circ$, which is how a too-fat or too-thin triangle produces a pathological weight."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def cot(u, v):
    # cot of angle between u and v = (u . v) / |u x v|
    cr = u[0] * v[1] - u[1] * v[0]
    return np.dot(u, v) / abs(cr)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.2))

# --- Panel 1: one interior edge (i, j) and its two opposite angles alpha, beta.
ax = axes[0]
vi = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
vj = np.array([2.0, 0.0])
va = np.array([1.0, 1.3])   # opposite vertex in triangle above the edge
vb = np.array([1.0, -1.1])  # opposite vertex in triangle below the edge
tri_top = np.array([vi, vj, va, vi])
tri_bot = np.array([vi, vj, vb, vi])
ax.fill(tri_top[:, 0], tri_top[:, 1], color="#cfe3f7", zorder=0)
ax.fill(tri_bot[:, 0], tri_bot[:, 1], color="#f7dfcf", zorder=0)
ax.plot([vi[0], vj[0]], [vi[1], vj[1]], "k-", lw=3, zorder=2)
for p, q in [(vi, va), (vj, va), (vi, vb), (vj, vb)]:
    ax.plot([p[0], q[0]], [p[1], q[1]], "k-", lw=1, zorder=1)
for p in (vi, vj, va, vb):
    ax.plot(p[0], p[1], "ko", ms=6, zorder=3)
ax.annotate("$i$", vi, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-12, -2), fontsize=13)
ax.annotate("$j$", vj, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(6, -2), fontsize=13)
ax.annotate(r"$\alpha_{ij}$", va, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-10, 8), fontsize=13)
ax.annotate(r"$\beta_{ij}$", vb, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-10, -16), fontsize=13)
a_top = cot(vi - va, vj - va)
a_bot = cot(vi - vb, vj - vb)
w = 0.5 * (a_top + a_bot)
ax.set_title(r"Edge $(i,j)$:  $w_{ij}=\frac{1}{2}(\cot\alpha+\cot\beta)$"
             + f"\n$\\cot\\alpha={a_top:.2f}$, $\\cot\\beta={a_bot:.2f}$, $w_{{ij}}={w:.2f}$",
             fontsize=11)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_xlim(-0.6, 2.6); ax.set_ylim(-1.6, 1.8)

# --- Panel 2: the weight blowing up as the opposite angle -> 0 (sliver).
ax = axes[1]
ang = np.linspace(np.deg2rad(2), np.deg2rad(170), 400)
ax.plot(np.rad2deg(ang), 1.0 / np.tan(ang), color="#b2182b", lw=2)
ax.axhline(0.0, color="k", lw=0.8)
ax.axvline(90.0, color="gray", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("opposite angle (degrees)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\cot(\mathrm{angle})$")
ax.set_ylim(-6, 12)
ax.set_title("Cotangent weight blows up for a sliver\n(angle $\\to 0$) and goes negative past $90^\\circ$",
             fontsize=11)
ax.annotate("sliver:\nweight $\\to +\\infty$", (6, 9.5), fontsize=10, color="#b2182b")
ax.annotate("obtuse:\nnegative weight", (120, -4.5), fontsize=10, color="#2166ac")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Why the cotangent: the finite-element derivation

The cotangent weight is not a heuristic that happens to behave well; it is the
exact coefficient that the principled construction produces. Cover the smooth
surface with the piecewise-linear hat basis functions $\varphi_i$ - one per vertex,
equal to $1$ at vertex $i$, $0$ at every other vertex, linear on each triangle -
and ask for the finite-element stiffness matrix of the Laplacian, whose entries are

$$
L_{ij} \;=\; \int_{\text{surface}} \nabla\varphi_i \cdot \nabla\varphi_j \; \mathrm{d}A.
$$ {#eq-fem}

Carrying out that integral over the flat triangles collapses the algebra to exactly
$-\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})$ off the diagonal. Equation
@eq-cotweight *is* @eq-fem evaluated by hand. This is why the cotangent Laplacian,
unlike the uniform one, converges to the smooth $\Delta$ under refinement: it is the
Galerkin discretisation of the true operator, so the convergence theory of finite
elements applies (the discretisation argument of
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-discretization)).

The derivation also makes the operator's symmetries transparent, and these are the
inductive bias that recommends $\Delta$ for surface analysis. The integrand
$\nabla\varphi_i\cdot\nabla\varphi_j\,\mathrm{d}A$ is built from gradients and an
area element, both of which depend only on the *intrinsic metric* - the edge
lengths and angles of the triangulation - and not on how the surface is positioned
in space. Consequently $L$ is invariant to rigid motion of the embedding and, more
strikingly, to any *isometric* bending that preserves the metric: flatten a cortical
patch without stretching it and the cotangent weights are unchanged. The operator is
intrinsic. A model whose only access to a surface is through $\Delta$ therefore
cannot depend on the surface's pose, on its embedding, or on the arbitrary choice of
triangulation - those degrees of freedom have been quotiented away before any
learning or estimation begins. This is the geometric counterpart of building
translation equivariance into a grid convolution: the symmetry is in the operator,
not something a downstream model must rediscover from data.

### Sign, definiteness, and what `nitrix` returns

Two conventions are worth pinning down.

- **Sign.** The continuous Laplacian is negative-semidefinite ($\nabla^2$ of a bump
  is negative at the peak). The form in @eq-cotlap, with positive diagonal, is its
  negation $-\Delta$, which is positive-semidefinite: all eigenvalues $\ge 0$, the
  smallest exactly $0$ on the constants. `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` returns this PSD
  form (its docstring is explicit: it returns $-\Delta$; negate the `values` for the
  negative form). PSD is the convention every solver that touches it - conjugate
  gradients, eigensolvers - expects.
- **Storage.** $L$ is sparse, with one nonzero per 1-ring neighbour plus the
  diagonal. `nitrix` stores it as an ELL operator, or a degree-bucketed
  `SectionedELL` for irregular-valence meshes, the padded sparse format from
  [the operators chapter](operators.qmd#sec-ell). The flat ELL uses a diagonal-first
  column layout (column 0 is always $L_{ii}$), a detail the Neumann-ROI restriction
  of @sec-smoothing relies on.

### `mesh_cotangent_laplacian`: execution class and assembly

`mesh_cotangent_laplacian(mesh, *, format='ell')` is **HOST-CTOR**:
data-dependent construction that runs on the host in NumPy and emits a plain-JAX
ELL. It casts `mesh.vertices` to float64 for the angle arithmetic (cotangents of
near-degenerate triangles are numerically delicate, so assembly is done in double
precision and the result cast back to the mesh dtype), computes a
per-`(face, opposite-vertex)` cotangent via `_cotangent_weights`, then assembles the
sparse rows by a fully vectorised scatter. It builds the six directed $(i,j,w)$
contributions - both orientations of each of a face's three opposite edges - folds
duplicate edges together with a single integer key $\text{key}=i\cdot n + j$ and
`np.add.at`, accumulates the diagonal as the row sum, and lays the off-diagonals into
the ELL slots. There is no Python per-face loop and no dictionary.

Because the weights are baked in as constant array values, the resulting ELL is the
right tool for applying a *fixed* operator to *many* fields - precisely the smoothing
case. It is not differentiable with respect to the vertex positions, because the host
construction lies outside the JAX trace. The rule from
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-classes) applies directly:

> Never call a HOST op inside a jitted loop. Build `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` once,
> on the host, and reuse the ELL across every smoothing solve or matvec.
> Re-assembling it inside a `jit`/`fori_loop` would either fail to trace or silently
> recompile every step.

When instead the Laplacian's action must be differentiable through the geometry -
when the vertices are being optimised and the weights must move with them - there is
a separate JAX-native path, `_cotangent_apply(vertices, faces, field)`, in
`geometry.surface`. It computes the same $(Lf)[i]=\sum_j w_{ij}(f[i]-f[j])$ by
per-face assembly entirely in JAX, so it is **DIFF-JAX** (jittable and differentiable
with respect to `vertices`), at the cost of recomputing the weights on every call.
The two paths agree numerically; one chooses the path whose execution class matches
the task. `_cotangent_apply` is the engine of mean curvature on the
[next page](curvature.qmd#sec-mean); `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` is the engine of
smoothing here.

## The mass matrix {#sec-mass}

### Why $L$ alone is not the operator

There is a subtlety in @eq-fem. The stiffness $L$ is an integral over the surface, so
its rows carry units of area; it is the *unnormalised* operator. It reports the
total, area-weighted imbalance of $f$ around each vertex, but it has not divided by
how much surface each vertex represents. A vertex in a region of large triangles owns
more surface than a vertex in a finely tessellated patch, and the true differential
operator must account for this.

The accountant is the mass matrix $M$, the discrete inner product on fields. For two
per-vertex fields $f,g$ the surface integral $\int f\,g\,\mathrm{d}A$ is approximated
by $f^\top M g$, so $M$ encodes how much area each vertex stands in for. The genuine
Laplace--Beltrami operator is then

$$
\Delta \;=\; M^{-1}L,
$$ {#eq-lbo}

stiffness divided by mass: $L f$ is the area-weighted imbalance, and $M^{-1}$ converts
it back to a per-unit-area rate of change, a true second derivative along the surface.
The generalised eigenproblem of the operator is $L\phi_k=\lambda_k M\phi_k$ (the
subject of [the spectral chapter](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen)); the smoothing solve is
$(M+tL)x=Mx_0$ (@sec-smoothing). In both, $M$ and $L$ appear as separate matrices the
algorithm combines, which is how `nitrix` ships them.

The split also exposes a scale symmetry worth naming. Under a uniform scaling of the
surface by a factor $s$, angles are unchanged, so the cotangent weights and $L$ are
*invariant*; areas scale by $s^2$, so $M$ scales by $s^2$. Hence $\Delta=M^{-1}L$
scales by $s^{-2}$, the correct equivariance for an operator with units of inverse
length squared - the same scaling that gives mass-normalised curvature its units of
inverse length. The operator transforms the way the geometry demands, automatically,
because stiffness and mass carry their units separately.

### Lumped mass and the two area schemes

The fully consistent FEM mass matrix has off-diagonal entries, since neighbouring hat
functions overlap. `nitrix` ships the lumped approximation: collapse each row onto its
diagonal, giving a diagonal matrix $M=\operatorname{diag}(A_i)$ where $A_i$ is the
surface area attributed to vertex $i$. Lumping costs a little accuracy but makes
$M^{-1}$ the elementwise reciprocal $1/A_i$, invertible at a glance, which is exactly
what the $M^{-1}L$ curvature and the $(M+tL)$ smoothing solve need.
(`mesh_mass_matrix(mesh, *, scheme='voronoi', lumped=True)` raises
`NotImplementedError` for `lumped=False`: the consistent mass is a documented
follow-up, not yet built.)

The remaining question is how to split each triangle's area among its three vertices.
`nitrix` offers two schemes through `vertex_areas(mesh, *, scheme=...)`, both of which
*partition* the triangle exactly, so for either scheme $\sum_i A_i = \sum_f
\text{area}_f$, the total surface area, with no area lost or double-counted.

- **`scheme='barycentric'`** donates $\text{area}/3$ from each face to each of its
  three vertices. Cheap, always non-negative, never pathological - the safe fallback.
- **`scheme='voronoi'`** (the default) is the mixed-Voronoi rule of Meyer et al.
  (2003). For a non-obtuse triangle, vertex $i$ receives its circumcentric-dual
  (Voronoi) area,
  $$
  A_i^{\triangle} \;=\; \tfrac18\bigl(\cot\theta_k\,\lVert x_i-x_j\rVert^2
  + \cot\theta_j\,\lVert x_i-x_k\rVert^2\bigr),
  $$ {#eq-voronoi}
  the portion of the triangle closer to $x_i$ than to the other two vertices, with
  $\theta_j,\theta_k$ the angles at those other two vertices. This is the
  geometrically faithful partition. For an obtuse triangle the circumcenter falls
  outside the triangle, @eq-voronoi can go negative, and the Voronoi cell is no longer
  contained in the triangle, so Meyer's rule switches to a safe fallback: the obtuse
  vertex receives $\text{area}/2$ and the other two receive $\text{area}/4$ each.

@fig-lbo-voronoi draws all three cases. The obtuse branch is not academic. It is
load-bearing on real cortical surfaces, which are riddled with obtuse triangles, and
it is exactly the case the barycentric scheme silently mishandles. The icosphere,
near-equilateral everywhere, never exercises the obtuse branch - which is precisely
why an icosphere oracle, useful as it is for *convergence* checks, cannot validate
this code path; that requires a real mesh.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lbo-voronoi
#| fig-cap: "Three ways to attribute triangle area to vertices. Left: barycentric — split at the centroid, each vertex gets area/3. Middle: mixed-Voronoi on an acute triangle — the cells meet at the circumcenter, which lies *inside* the triangle, and each vertex gets its circumcentric-dual area. Right: an obtuse triangle — the circumcenter escapes the triangle, so Meyer's rule falls back to area/2 for the obtuse vertex and area/4 for the others."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def circumcenter(A, B, C):
    ax, ay = A; bx, by = B; cx, cy = C
    d = 2 * (ax * (by - cy) + bx * (cy - ay) + cx * (ay - by))
    ux = ((ax**2 + ay**2) * (by - cy) + (bx**2 + by**2) * (cy - ay)
          + (cx**2 + cy**2) * (ay - by)) / d
    uy = ((ax**2 + ay**2) * (cx - bx) + (bx**2 + by**2) * (ax - cx)
          + (cx**2 + cy**2) * (bx - ax)) / d
    return np.array([ux, uy])

def midpoint(P, Q):
    return 0.5 * (P + Q)

def draw_voronoi(ax, A, B, C, title):
    tri = np.array([A, B, C, A])
    ax.fill(tri[:, 0], tri[:, 1], color="#eeeeee", zorder=0)
    cc = circumcenter(A, B, C)
    mAB, mBC, mCA = midpoint(A, B), midpoint(B, C), midpoint(C, A)
    cells = {"A": np.array([A, mAB, cc, mCA]),
             "B": np.array([B, mBC, cc, mAB]),
             "C": np.array([C, mCA, cc, mBC])}
    colors = {"A": "#cfe3f7", "B": "#f7dfcf", "C": "#d6f5d6"}
    for k, poly in cells.items():
        p = np.vstack([poly, poly[0]])
        ax.fill(p[:, 0], p[:, 1], color=colors[k], zorder=1, alpha=0.9)
        ax.plot(p[:, 0], p[:, 1], color="gray", lw=0.8, zorder=2)
    ax.plot(tri[:, 0], tri[:, 1], "k-", lw=2, zorder=3)
    ax.plot(cc[0], cc[1], "ko", ms=5, zorder=4)
    ax.annotate("circum-\ncenter", cc, textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(6, 4), fontsize=9)
    for P, name in [(A, "A"), (B, "B"), (C, "C")]:
        ax.plot(P[0], P[1], "ko", ms=5, zorder=4)
        ax.annotate(name, P, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-10, -10), fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4.4))

# Panel 1: barycentric -- each vertex gets area/3, split at the centroid.
ax = axes[0]
A = np.array([0.0, 0.0]); B = np.array([2.2, 0.0]); C = np.array([0.8, 1.8])
cen = (A + B + C) / 3.0
tri = np.array([A, B, C, A])
ax.fill(tri[:, 0], tri[:, 1], color="#eeeeee", zorder=0)
mAB, mBC, mCA = midpoint(A, B), midpoint(B, C), midpoint(C, A)
for P, m1, m2, col in [(A, mAB, mCA, "#cfe3f7"),
                       (B, mBC, mAB, "#f7dfcf"),
                       (C, mCA, mBC, "#d6f5d6")]:
    poly = np.array([P, m1, cen, m2, P])
    ax.fill(poly[:, 0], poly[:, 1], color=col, zorder=1)
    ax.plot(poly[:, 0], poly[:, 1], color="gray", lw=0.8, zorder=2)
ax.plot(tri[:, 0], tri[:, 1], "k-", lw=2, zorder=3)
ax.plot(cen[0], cen[1], "ko", ms=5, zorder=4)
for P, name in [(A, "A"), (B, "B"), (C, "C")]:
    ax.annotate(name, P, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-10, -10), fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Barycentric: each vertex gets\narea/3 (split at the centroid)", fontsize=11)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")

# Panel 2: Voronoi (acute) -- circumcentric dual cells inside the triangle.
draw_voronoi(axes[1], np.array([0.0, 0.0]), np.array([2.2, 0.0]),
             np.array([1.1, 1.7]),
             "Mixed-Voronoi (acute): cells meet\nat the circumcenter, inside the triangle")

# Panel 3: obtuse triangle -- circumcenter outside; fallback A/2, A/4, A/4.
ax = axes[2]
A = np.array([0.0, 0.0]); B = np.array([3.0, 0.0]); C = np.array([2.6, 0.9])
tri = np.array([A, B, C, A])
cc = circumcenter(A, B, C)
ax.fill(tri[:, 0], tri[:, 1], color="#fde0dd", zorder=0)
ax.plot(tri[:, 0], tri[:, 1], "k-", lw=2, zorder=3)
ax.plot(cc[0], cc[1], "rx", ms=9, mew=2, zorder=4)
ax.annotate("circumcenter\noutside!", cc, textcoords="offset points",
            xytext=(-30, 8), fontsize=9, color="#b2182b")
for P, name in [(A, "A:  A/2"), (B, "B:  A/4"), (C, "C:  A/4")]:
    ax.annotate(name, P, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-6, -14), fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Obtuse triangle: Voronoi cell escapes\nthe triangle -> fallback A/2 (obtuse), A/4, A/4",
             fontsize=11)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 3.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The supporting routines: `face_areas`, `vertex_areas`, `mesh_mass_matrix`

Three public symbols build the mass, all **DIFF-JAX** (pure JAX, differentiable with
respect to `mesh.vertices`):

- `face_areas(mesh)` $\to$ `(n_faces,)`. Half the magnitude of the face cross
  product, $\text{area}_f=\tfrac12\lVert(v_1-v_0)\times(v_2-v_0)\rVert$. The atom the
  other two build on.
- `vertex_areas(mesh, *, scheme='voronoi')` $\to$ `(n_vertices,)`. The lumped-mass
  diagonal $A_i$, by the two schemes above. This is the object nearly every shipped
  algorithm consumes: `mean_curvature` forms $\tfrac12 Lv/A$, `surface_smooth` forms
  $A\,v+t\,Lv$, the spectral embedding uses $1/\sqrt{A}$.
- `mesh_mass_matrix(mesh, *, scheme='voronoi', lumped=True)` $\to$ a width-1 diagonal
  ELL, $M_{ii}=A_i$, so $M$ can flow through the same
  [`apply_operator`](operators.qmd#sec-apply) seam as $L$ ($Mx=A_i x$). This is a
  convenient symmetry but not a load-bearing seam: as the source docstring notes, the
  shipped algorithms take the `vertex_areas` *vector* directly. Reach for
  `vertex_areas` in ordinary code; reach for `mesh_mass_matrix` only when you genuinely
  want $M$ as an operator alongside $L$.

The architectural point bears repeating, because it shapes the whole API: $L$ and $M$
are separate symbols. There is no `normalize=True` flag on the Laplacian. The algorithm
that needs $\Delta$ forms $M^{-1}L$; the algorithm that needs a heat step forms $M+tL$;
the eigensolver forms the pencil $(L,M)$. Keeping them apart is what lets one stiffness
and one mass serve curvature, smoothing, and the spectrum without a combinatorial
explosion of normalisation options.

## First application: geodesic (heat) smoothing {#sec-smoothing}

We can now answer the motivating problem, using $L$ and $M$ together for the first
time.

### Smoothing as heat diffusion

The bridge is a fact from physics: Gaussian smoothing and heat diffusion are the same
operation. Let a field diffuse - obey the heat equation $\partial_t u = -\Delta u$,
with our PSD sign - for a time $t$, and the result is the field convolved with a
Gaussian of variance $\sigma^2=2t$. On a flat domain this is the ordinary blur; on a
surface, diffusing with the Laplace--Beltrami $\Delta$ produces exactly the geodesic
smoothing we want, with heat flowing along the cortex rather than across the space
between folds. So to smooth a map by a target FWHM, we diffuse it for the matching
time. The Gaussian relation $\text{FWHM}=2\sqrt{2\ln 2}\,\sigma$ with $\sigma^2=2t$
inverts to the calibration

$$
t \;=\; \frac{\text{FWHM}^2}{16\ln 2}.
$$ {#eq-fwhm}

That is the one knob the user sets, in the mesh's coordinate units; everything else is
solving for the diffused field.

### Explicit versus implicit time stepping

How do we evolve the field for time $t$? The explicit (forward-Euler) recipe is
$x \leftarrow x - \tau M^{-1}L x$ in small time-steps $\tau$. It works, but only
conditionally: explicit diffusion is stable only for $\tau$ below a limit set by the
smallest triangle, so a mesh with a few slivers forces thousands of microscopic steps.
The implicit (backward-Euler) scheme removes the restriction. Evaluate the diffusion at
the *end* of the step, $x \leftarrow x - \tau M^{-1}L x_{\text{new}}$, rearrange to
$(M + \tau L)x_{\text{new}} = M x$, and the scheme becomes unconditionally stable - any
step size is allowed. A single backward-Euler step of the full time $t$ therefore
realises the whole diffusion at once,

$$
(M + tL)\,x \;=\; M\,x_0,
$$ {#eq-backeuler}

with $x_0$ the input field, $x$ the smoothed output, $M=\operatorname{diag}(A_i)$ the
lumped mass, $L$ the cotangent stiffness, and $t$ from @eq-fwhm. The system matrix
$M+tL$ is symmetric positive-definite (mass is positive, stiffness is PSD, the sum is
SPD), exactly the class the next ingredient solves.

One caveat, which the source is candid about: backward Euler is unconditionally stable
but only first-order accurate in $t$. A single giant step is a Padé approximation
$(I+tM^{-1}L)^{-1}$ to the true heat propagator $\exp(-tM^{-1}L)$; for the smooth maps
it is built for, the difference is negligible, but for a perfectly sharp delta the
single step under-spreads. Splitting the time into $K$ sub-steps,
$(I+\tfrac{t}{K}M^{-1}L)^{-K}$, converges to the exact Gaussian as $K$ grows - a clean
instance of the unit's convergence-under-refinement theme, demonstrated in
@fig-lbo-smoothing. `surface_smooth` takes the single-step path, the right trade for
real, already band-limited cortical maps.

### Solving it matrix-free with `cg`

We must solve @eq-backeuler. We do not want to factorise $M+tL$: on the GPU that means
a Cholesky through the cuSolver handle pool, the path
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-devices) flags as wedge-prone, and it would force
materialising the matrix. Because $M+tL$ is SPD and we only ever need its *action* on a
vector - $v\mapsto M v + t\,Lv = A_i v + t\,(Lv)$ - the natural solver is conjugate
gradients, built from matvecs and inner products alone, with no factorisation and no
cuSolver.

`linalg.krylov.cg(a, b, *, x0=None, tol=1e-6, atol=0.0, maxiter=None, l2=0.0)` is that
solver (**DIFF-JAX**). Its first argument `a` is either a dense SPD matrix or a callable
computing the matvec $v\mapsto Av$, and the matrix-free callable form is the one
`surface_smooth` uses, so $M+tL$ is never materialised. It wraps
`jax.scipy.sparse.linalg.cg`, which carries its own implicit-differentiation rule:
gradients flow through the solve without unrolling the iterations, so `surface_smooth`
is differentiable with respect to the field `values`. The `l2` argument adds a Tikhonov
ridge to the diagonal (matrix-free, $Av+\ell_2 v$) and may be a *traced* scalar so the
damping can vary inside a `jit` - the seam the registration normal-equations solve
reuses, though smoothing leaves it at $0$. CG converges in at most $n$ exact steps for
an $n\times n$ SPD system; `maxiter` defaults to JAX's $10n$, `tol` to a relative
residual of $10^{-6}$.

### `surface_smooth`: putting it together

`surface_smooth(mesh, values, *, fwhm, area_scheme='voronoi', roi=None, tol=1e-6,
maxiter=None)` is the public geodesic smoother, and its body is the whole chapter in a
dozen lines.

1. `area = vertex_areas(mesh, scheme=area_scheme)` - the lumped mass diagonal $M$
   (@sec-mass).
2. `lap = mesh_cotangent_laplacian(mesh)` - the stiffness $L$, built once on the host
   (HOST-CTOR, @sec-cotangent).
3. `t = fwhm**2 / (16 * log 2)` - the FWHM-to-time calibration, @eq-fwhm.
4. Define the matvec `_matvec(v) = area * v + t * apply_operator(lap, v)`, i.e.
   $v\mapsto(M+tL)v$, threading $L$ through the format-agnostic
   [`apply_operator`](operators.qmd#sec-apply) seam so the same code serves a flat or
   sectioned ELL.
5. `return cg(_matvec, area * values, ...)` - solve @eq-backeuler with right-hand side
   $M x_0$, matrix-free.

It accepts a batched `values` of shape `(..., n_vertices)` (vertex axis last), so many
maps smooth at once, and `fwhm=0` is the identity. Because $\mathbf{1}^\top L=0$ (every
column of $L$ sums to zero, the transpose of the zero-row-sum property), the smoother
conserves the area-weighted integral $\sum_i A_i x_i$: no signal is created or
destroyed, only redistributed, and constants are fixed exactly. That conservation law
is a free correctness oracle to assert in a test - the smoothing analogue of "the row
sums are zero," and itself a symmetry statement (the smoother commutes with adding a
constant and preserves total mass).

### The medial-wall guard: `_roi_neumann_laplacian`

Real cortical maps carry a medial wall - the non-cortical patch where the hemisphere
surfaces meet - whose vertex values are meaningless and often huge or NaN. Smoothing
naively lets that junk bleed across the boundary into good cortex. The fix is a Neumann
(no-flux) boundary at the ROI edge: heat is not permitted to flow across it.

`_roi_neumann_laplacian(lap, roi)` implements this by editing the stiffness ELL. The
idea is to drop every edge that crosses the boundary and then repair the diagonal so
each surviving row still sums to zero - because a zero-row-sum row passes no net flux,
dropping the cross-boundary couplings and rebuilding the diagonal from only the
surviving in-ROI weights produces a no-flux wall. Concretely it keeps an off-diagonal
entry only when both endpoints lie in the ROI (`roi[i] & roi[idx] & is_offdiag`, where
`is_offdiag` masks out column 0 - this is why the diagonal-first ELL layout matters),
sets each in-ROI diagonal to minus the sum of its surviving off-diagonals, and zeroes
out-of-ROI rows entirely so the mass term alone holds those vertices at their input.
(`surface_smooth` requires a flat `ELL` for ROI masking - it raises on a `SectionedELL`
- and drives the CG solve from the ROI data alone, restoring out-of-ROI vertices
afterward, so meaningless medial-wall magnitudes never inflate $\lVert b\rVert$ and
starve the ROI of precision.) This is the `wb_command -metric-smoothing -roi` guard,
native to JAX. One documented divergence: `surface_smooth` is the heat-diffusion
geodesic smoother, not byte-identical to Connectome Workbench's
geodesic-*distance*-weighted Gaussian - the same intent, a different kernel, validated
by class and correlation rather than bit-parity (`docs/design/geometry-suite.md`).

```{python}
#| label: fig-lbo-smoothing
#| fig-cap: "Geodesic smoothing on a flat triangulated patch (where geodesic = Euclidean, so the analytic oracle is a planar Gaussian of known FWHM). Left: the measured FWHM of a smoothed delta converges to the requested value as the heat flow is split into more backward-Euler sub-steps — one step under-spreads a sharp delta (first-order accuracy), $K$ steps approach the exact Gaussian. Right: the converged profile, with measured-vs-requested FWHM and the conserved area-weighted mass before/after the solve."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# --- Build a flat triangulated square patch in the z=0 plane. ---------------
G = 33
L_side = 8.0
xs = np.linspace(-L_side / 2, L_side / 2, G)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, xs, indexing="ij")
V = np.stack([X.ravel(), Y.ravel(), np.zeros(G * G)], axis=1)
def vid(i, j):
    return i * G + j
faces = []
for i in range(G - 1):
    for j in range(G - 1):
        faces.append([vid(i, j), vid(i + 1, j), vid(i, j + 1)])
        faces.append([vid(i + 1, j), vid(i + 1, j + 1), vid(i, j + 1)])
F = np.array(faces, dtype=np.int64)
n = V.shape[0]

# --- Assemble the cotangent stiffness L (PSD) and barycentric lumped mass. ---
def cot_at(p, q, r):
    u = q - p; v = r - p
    cr = np.linalg.norm(np.cross(u, v))
    return np.dot(u, v) / max(cr, 1e-30)

Lmat = np.zeros((n, n))
area = np.zeros(n)
for (a, b, c) in F:
    A, B, C = V[a], V[b], V[c]
    tri_area = 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(np.cross(B - A, C - A))
    for v in (a, b, c):
        area[v] += tri_area / 3.0
    for (p, i, j) in ((a, b, c), (b, c, a), (c, a, b)):
        w = 0.5 * cot_at(V[p], V[i], V[j])  # edge (i, j), opposite p
        Lmat[i, i] += w; Lmat[j, j] += w
        Lmat[i, j] -= w; Lmat[j, i] -= w

# --- Backward-Euler heat flow to time t, split into K sub-steps. -------------
fwhm_req = 2.0
t = fwhm_req**2 / (16.0 * np.log(2.0))   # sigma^2 = 2t calibration
x0 = np.zeros(n)
x0[vid(G // 2, G // 2)] = 1.0 / area[vid(G // 2, G // 2)]  # unit-mass delta
M = np.diag(area)

def be_flow(K):
    """K backward-Euler sub-steps of total diffusion time t (one heat step
    operator, reused K times)."""
    step = np.linalg.inv(M + (t / K) * Lmat) @ M
    x = x0.copy()
    for _ in range(K):
        x = step @ x
    return x

def measure_fwhm(x):
    prof = np.clip(x.reshape(G, G)[G // 2, :], 0, None)
    half = prof.max() / 2.0
    above = np.where(prof >= half)[0]
    def cross(lo, hi):
        y0, y1 = prof[lo], prof[hi]
        return xs[lo] + (half - y0) / (y1 - y0) * (xs[hi] - xs[lo])
    return cross(above[0] - 1, above[0]), cross(above[-1], above[-1] + 1), prof

Ks = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16]
fwhms = []
for K in Ks:
    xK = be_flow(K)
    xL, xR, _ = measure_fwhm(xK)
    fwhms.append(xR - xL)

x_fine = be_flow(Ks[-1])
mass0 = float(area @ x0)
mass1 = float(area @ x_fine)
xL, xR, prof = measure_fwhm(x_fine)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.4))
axes[0].plot(Ks, fwhms, "o-", color="#2166ac")
axes[0].axhline(fwhm_req, color="#b2182b", ls="--", lw=1,
                label=f"requested FWHM = {fwhm_req:.1f}")
axes[0].set_xscale("log", base=2)
axes[0].set_xlabel("backward-Euler sub-steps $K$")
axes[0].set_ylabel("measured FWHM")
axes[0].set_title("Calibration converges under sub-stepping\n"
                  r"$(I+\frac{t}{K}L)^{-K}\to e^{-tL}$", fontsize=11)
axes[0].legend(fontsize=9)

axes[1].plot(xs, prof, "o-", ms=3, color="#2166ac", label="smoothed profile")
axes[1].axhline(prof.max() / 2.0, color="gray", ls=":", lw=1)
axes[1].axvline(xL, color="#b2182b", ls="--", lw=1)
axes[1].axvline(xR, color="#b2182b", ls="--", lw=1)
axes[1].set_xlim(-3, 3)
axes[1].set_xlabel("x (mesh units)"); axes[1].set_ylabel("value")
axes[1].set_title(
    f"K={Ks[-1]}: requested {fwhm_req:.2f}, measured {xR - xL:.2f}\n"
    f"mass before {mass0:.4f}, after {mass1:.4f} (conserved)",
    fontsize=11)
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Summary and what follows

The cotangent stiffness $L$ (@sec-cotangent) and the lumped mass $M$ (@sec-mass)
compose into the discrete Laplace--Beltrami operator $\Delta=M^{-1}L$, and we have seen
them combine in two ways: $M^{-1}L$ for differential quantities and $M+tL$ for
diffusion. Both are intrinsic - determined by the metric, invariant to rigid motion and
to the choice of triangulation, equivariant under scaling and under vertex relabelling -
which is exactly the inductive bias that makes $\Delta$ the natural operator for surface
analysis. The same two pieces drive the next two chapters: curvature reads $\tfrac12
M^{-1}L$ applied to the vertex *positions* ([the mean-curvature chapter](curvature.qmd#sec-mean),
where the differentiable `_cotangent_apply` path is the engine), and the surface
spectrum solves the generalised eigenproblem $L\phi_k=\lambda_k M\phi_k$
([the spectral chapter](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen)). The full uniform-versus-cotangent,
graph-versus-metric contrast - when the connectivity-only graph Laplacian is the right
choice and when it is not - is taken up under
[graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-graph-laplacian). For the solver machinery behind
the smoothing step, see [the conjugate-gradient section of the solvers chapter](solvers.qmd#sec-cg).

## Further reading

- Pinkall, U., & Polthier, K. (1993). *Computing discrete minimal surfaces and their
  conjugates*. **Experimental Mathematics**, 2(1), 15–36. The origin of the cotangent
  weight as a discrete Dirichlet-energy / minimal-surface coefficient.
- Meyer, M., Desbrun, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (2003). *Discrete
  differential-geometry operators for triangulated 2-manifolds*. In **Visualization and
  Mathematics III** (pp. 35–57). Springer. The mixed-Voronoi mass and the
  curvature-normal operator nitrix implements.
- Desbrun, M., Meyer, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (1999). *Implicit fairing of
  irregular meshes using diffusion and curvature flow*. In **SIGGRAPH '99**
  (pp. 317–324). The backward-Euler heat-smoothing scheme of @sec-smoothing.
- Crane, K. (2018). *Discrete Differential Geometry: An Applied Introduction*. Lecture
  notes, Carnegie Mellon University. The most accessible modern derivation of $L$, $M$,
  and $\Delta=M^{-1}L$ from first principles.
- Botsch, M., Kobbelt, L., Pauly, M., Alliez, P., & Lévy, B. (2010). *Polygon Mesh
  Processing*. A K Peters / CRC Press. Standard reference for the FEM view of mesh
  operators and the smoothing/fairing pipeline.
- Reuter, M., Wolter, F.-E., & Peinecke, N. (2006). *Laplace–Beltrami spectra as
  "Shape-DNA" of surfaces and solids*. **Computer-Aided Design**, 38(4), 342–366. The
  generalised eigenproblem $L\phi=\lambda M\phi$ the
  [spectral chapter](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen) builds on.

## See also

- [`src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)
  — `mesh_cotangent_laplacian`, `mesh_mass_matrix`, `vertex_areas`, `face_areas`,
  `mesh_laplacian_smooth`.
- [`src/nitrix/geometry/surface.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/surface.py)
  — `surface_smooth`, `_cotangent_apply`, `_roi_neumann_laplacian`.
- [`src/nitrix/linalg/krylov.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/krylov.py)
  — `cg`, the matrix-free SPD solver behind the heat step.
- [`src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py)
  — `apply_operator`, the format-agnostic seam threading $L$ and $M$ into the matvec.
- [`docs/feature-requests/mesh-curvature.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/feature-requests/mesh-curvature.md)
  — the curvature primitives that consume $M^{-1}L$.
- Neighbouring pages: [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-discretization) ·
  [operators](operators.qmd#sec-ell) · [curvature](curvature.qmd#sec-mean) ·
  [spectral](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen) · [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-cg) ·
  [graph geometry](graph-geometry.qmd#sec-graph-laplacian).
